era_context: The mid-10th century saw the fragmentation of the Abbasid Caliphate and the rise of regional Islamic dynasties. The Byzantine Empire under Constantine VII was at its cultural and diplomatic peak. The Northern Song Dynasty was consolidating control over China. In Europe, feudalism was becoming dominant. The Rus' were developing their state structure.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern and Western Europe (limited written sources)

events.title: Constantine VII Porphyrogennetos: Reign and Cultural Authority
events.description: In 956, Constantine VII continued to reign as Byzantine Emperor, maintaining diplomatic relations with neighboring powers and overseeing cultural and intellectual activities. His reign was marked by the compilation of important administrative and ceremonial texts, including the De Administrando Imperio and the De Ceremoniis. Constantine VII's court was a center of learning and diplomatic activity.

events.title: Northern Song Dynasty: Continued Reunification
events.description: In 956, the Northern Song Dynasty under Emperor Taizu continued its military campaigns to reunify China. The Song forces were consolidating control over southern territories. This period saw the development of Song military technology and administrative systems that would establish Song dominance over all of China by the early 960s.

sources.name: Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Song consolidation is well-documented. Specific events in 956 are less precisely dated.

events.title: Fatimid Caliphate: Expansion and Consolidation
events.description: In 956, the Fatimid Caliphate under al-Muizz li-Din Allah continued to expand its power and consolidate control over North Africa, Sicily, and the Levantine coast. The Fatimids were establishing themselves as a major Mediterranean power, challenging both Byzantine and Abbasid authority.
events.certainty_note: Fatimid expansion during this period is well-attested, though specific events in 956 are less precisely documented.

events.title: Kievan Rus': State Development and Diplomatic Activity
events.description: In 956, the Kievan Rus' under Prince Igor I (or his regent, possibly Olga) continued to develop their state structure and maintain diplomatic relations with neighboring powers, including the Byzantine Empire. The Rus' were consolidating their control over eastern European territories.
key_figures: Olga of Kiev (regent, if still living)
events.certainty_note: Early Kievan Rus' chronology is uncertain. The Primary Chronicle is the main source but was compiled in the 12th century and contains legendary material.

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of early Kievan Rus' in 956 is highly disputed. The Primary Chronicle's account should not be treated as precise historical documentation for this period. Modern scholars debate the dating of key events and figures by ±10–20 years.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 956 is moderate. Byzantine sources are rich and contemporary. Islamic sources are abundant but often compiled later. Chinese sources are systematic and detailed. Early Rus' sources are problematic and legendary. Western European sources are sparse. Non-literate regions have no written records for this year.

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
